coredata.dc:title: Boosting carrier dynamics of BiVO4 photoanode via heterostructuring with ultrathin BiOI nanosheets for enhanced solar water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Can
Feng, Fan
Jian, Jie
Xu, Youxun
Li, Fan
Wang, Hongqiang
Jia, Lichao
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has been one of the most promising candidates for solar water splitting while still suffers from poor bulk charge transport that limits its solar to hydrogen conversion efficiency. We demonstrate in this work an efficient strategy for boosting bulk charge transport of BiVO4 through the facile impregnation of as-prepared BiVO4 photoanode in the precursor solution of ultrathin BiOI nanosheets. Such impregnation creates increased oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 through the reduction of V5+ to V4+ by I−, which greatly improves bulk separation efficiency for BiVO4-BiOI up to 65.9 % at 1.23 VRHE from the original 51.9 % of pure BiVO4. Moreover, the decoration of the BiOI nanosheets on BiVO4 photoanode is also beneficial for addressing the carrier dynamics at surface due to the matched energy levels of BiOI nanosheets and BiVO4. The introduced plenty of oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 and the built-in electric field in BiVO4-BiOI synergistically improve the photocurrent density at 1.23 VRHE up to 3.88 mA cm-2. We believe that such facile impregnation strategy will pave an alternative way to the development of highly efficient BiVO4 photoanode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth vanadate
BiOI nanosheets
Heterojunction
Solution impregnation
Oxygen vacancy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. 3102019JC005 , 3102019ghxm004 , GK201702007 and G2017KY0002 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51872179 , 51911530212 , 51872240 , 51672225 , and 11811530635 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province ( 2017JM5028 and 2020JM-273 ), the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing (NPU), China (Grant No. 2019-QZ-03 ) and the 1000 Youth Talent Program of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top view (a) and side view (b) SEM images of BiVO4 photoanode; Top view (c) and side view (d) SEM images of BiVO4-BiOI photoanode; X-ray diffraction patterns (e) and UV–vis absorption spectra (f) of BiVO4 (blue curve) and BiVO4-BiOI (red curve) photoanodes.

(a) TEM images of BiVO4-BiOI sample; High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of red (b) and yellow (c) framed areas in (a); (d) STEM image of BiVO4-BiOI sample; (e-g) EDS element mapping images.
High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra of (a) Bi 4f, (b) V 2p, (c) I 3d and (d) O 1s for the BiVO4 (blue curve), BiVO4-BiOI (red curve) and BiOI (orange curve).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of BiVO4 film and BiOI film. The pictures inserted are the digital photograph of BiVO4 film (yellow one) and BiOI film (orange one); (b) Tauc plot of BiVO4 and BiOI; (c) Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy spectra of BiVO4 and BiOI. The dashed black lines are the baseline and the tangents of the curve respectively. (d) Band structure diagram for BiVO4 and BiOI. The short blue lines and orange lines mark the redox potential of water and sulfite oxidation potential respectively.

(a) Sulfite oxidation photocurrent density-potential curves of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes at 1.23 VRHE in 0.5 M KH2PO4 under illumination (100 mW cm−2); (c) Incident photo-to-current conversion efficiency curve of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes at the specified wavelength; (d) Mott-Schottky plots of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes measured in 0.5 M KH2PO4 with 1 M Na2SO3 at 1 kHz frequency; (e) bulk charge separation efficiency of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes; (f) 5-h-continuous sulfite oxidation measurement for BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes at 0.8 VRHE in fresh 0.5 M KH2PO4 with 1 M Na2SO3 for 5 runs under AM 1.5 G irradiation.
(a) Water oxidation photocurrent density-potential curves measured in 0.5 M KH2PO4 under illumination (100 mW cm−2) and (b) ABPE spectra of BiVO4/Co-Pi and BiVO4-BiOI/Co-Pi photoanodes.
Schematic illustration for the fabrication of BiVO4-BiOI photoanode by in-situ solution impregnation method and the formation mechanism of oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4.

head.ce:title: Boosting carrier dynamics of BiVO4 photoanode via heterostructuring with ultrathin BiOI nanosheets for enhanced solar water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemistry, National Ministry of Education, Shaanxi Key Laboratory for Advanced Energy Devices, Shaanxi Engineering Lab for Advanced Energy Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, 620 West Chang’an Street, Xi’an, Shaanxi, 710119, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center for Nano Energy Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University and Shaanxi Joint Labortary of Graphene, Xi’an, 710072, China

Northwestern Polytechnical University and Shaanxi Joint Labortary of Graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center for Nano Energy Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University and Shaanxi Joint Labortary of Graphene, Xi’an, 710072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has been one of the most promising candidates for solar water splitting while still suffers from poor bulk charge transport that limits its solar to hydrogen conversion efficiency. We demonstrate in this work an efficient strategy for boosting bulk charge transport of BiVO4 through the facile impregnation of as-prepared BiVO4 photoanode in the precursor solution of ultrathin BiOI nanosheets. Such impregnation creates increased oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 through the reduction of V5+ to V4+ by I−, which greatly improves bulk separation efficiency for BiVO4-BiOI up to 65.9 % at 1.23 VRHE from the original 51.9 % of pure BiVO4. Moreover, the decoration of the BiOI nanosheets on BiVO4 photoanode is also beneficial for addressing the carrier dynamics at surface due to the matched energy levels of BiOI nanosheets and BiVO4. The introduced plenty of oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 and the built-in electric field in BiVO4-BiOI synergistically improve the photocurrent density at 1.23 VRHE up to 3.88 mA cm-2. We believe that such facile impregnation strategy will pave an alternative way to the development of highly efficient BiVO4 photoanode.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is a promising technology to store solar energy in the form of hydrogen energy, which is an ideal clean energy source with high energy density and no contaminated by-products [1,2]. The solar-to-hydrogen conversation efficiency of PEC cells is directly dependent on the photoelectrodes. Various n-type (p-type) semiconductors have been developed as photoanodes (photocathodes) for PEC water oxidation (reduction) [3–7]. Among many metal oxide semiconductor photocatalysts, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a promising n-type semiconductor for photoanode owing to its appropriate bandgap (∼ 2.4 eV) [8–10], proper valence band position for oxygen evolution and favorable conduction band position located very near the hydrogen evolution potential [10–12]. However, the solar-to-hydrogen efficiency of pure BiVO4 is greatly limited by extensive photo-excited carrier recombination in the bulk of BiVO4, due to the poor carrier transport nature [13,14]. Various tactics based on the concept of surface decoration or bulk doping have been designed for enhanced photo-generated electron-hole separation [1,15–18]. Recently efforts proved that the intrinsic defect of oxygen vacancy could act as electron donor for increasing the majority carrier density and to improve charge separation [19–21]. Especially for the BiVO4 photoanode, it has been reported that partially reduce V5+ to V4+ by post-treatment strategies such as electrochemical reduction [21], chemical treatment [22] or hydrogenation [23], which could create oxygen vacancies and effectively boost the PEC performance. From these research results, it is easy to draw the conclusion that the treatment and modification of bismuth vanadate with a more reducing material could successfully introduce oxygen vacancies into BiVO4 and significantly improve the bulk separation efficiency.

Layered bismuth oxyhalides, p-type BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) have been proven to be good photocatalysts owing to the internal electric fields between the positive [Bi2O2]2+ slabs and negative double halogen atoms slabs, which could separate photo-generated carriers efficiently for degradation of organic compounds such as the rhodamine B [24].The reported band gap energies of BiOCl, BiOBr and BiOI are 3.22, 2.64 and 1.77 eV respectively [26], and the theoretical simulation results indicat that band structures of p-BiOI and n-BiVO4 match very well [24,27–29], thus the p-BiOI is a promising candidate to modify BiVO4 to alter the carrier dynamics at surfaces in terms of the matched energy band structure. Ye et al. had been successfully fabricated BiOI-BiVO4 photoanodes by electrodeposition with significantly improved PEC performance, which is mainly attributed to the formation of p-n junction with BiVO4 for increased surface charge transfer [29]. However, the lower bulk separation efficiency is still a major factor limiting the application of BiVO4 photoanode, such heterostructuring of BiVO4 with BiOI nanosheets for increased bulk carrier dynamics has rarely been explored.

In this work, we demonstrate an efficient strategy for boosting bulk charge transport through the facile impregnation of as-prepared BiVO4 photoanode in the precursor solution of ultrathin BiOI nanosheets. Such impregnation improves the photocurrent density of BiVO4-BiOI photoanode of 3.88 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE from 3.03 mA cm−2 of the pure BiVO4 photoanode. Further characterization and analysis indicated the enhanced PEC performance was mainly attributed to the increased carrier transport due to the abundant oxygen vacancies in BiVO4 and the efficient extraction of electron-hole pairs due to the built-in electric field existing in the BiVO4-BiOI heterojunction. Scheme 1 depicts the decoration of the ultrathin BiOI nanosheets on BiVO4 photoanode via the facile solution impregnation at room temperature, where the pentavalent vanadium could be reduced to quaternary vanadium by iodide anions, thus introducing abundant oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4. Simultaneously, the BiOI nanosheets can form heterojunction with BiVO4 nanoparticles, and the built-in electric field can form a synergistic effect with oxygen vacancies to promote the carrier dynamics of BiVO4.

Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, ≥98.0 %) and ethylene glycol (spectrophotometric grade, ≥99 %) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Glacial acetic acid (HPLC grade) and vanadyl (IV) acetylacetonate (C10H14O5V, 99 %) were obtained from Acros Organics and Fisher Chemical, respectively. Potassium iodide (KI, ≥99.0 %), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO ≥ 99.0 %), Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, ≥99.5 %), Sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, ≥97 %) and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96 %) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO, 14 Ω, 2.2 mm) glass substrates were produced by Nippon Sheet Glass Co., Ltd. All the FTO substrates were ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol, acetone and ethanol for 30 min respectively. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used for all solution preparations.

The BiVO4 photoanode was synthesized through the conversion of BiOI, and the BiOI film was firstly prepared on FTO by spin-coating. Typically, 5.25 mmol of BiNO3·5H2O was dissolved in the mixed solution of 5 ml acetic acid and 10 ml ethylene glycol, stirred at 30 °C for 1 h. Then, 5.25 mmol of KI was added into above solution and stirred for another 3 h at 30 °C. Finally, an orange transparent solution was obtained. Additionally, reducing the amount of BiNO3·5H2O and KI to 1.5 mmol the solution with the concentration of 0.1 M can be acquired.

The spin-coating process of BiOI film can be divided into two steps. First, 50 μL 0.1 M precursor solution was spin coated on a clean FTO substrate and then dried on a hot plate at 200 °C. After that the FTO substrate with thin layer precursor was immersed into the deionized water at 55 °C for 10 min to form the seed layer of BiOI, and then it was dried under nitrogen flow. Second, 50 μL of 0.35 M precursor solution was spin coated on the top of previous FTO substrate with BiOI seed layer. Following, the FTO substrate was placed in the deionized water at 55 °C and keeping for 30 s to ensure the formation of BiOI precipitation. Repeating the second step for 5 times, an orange BiOI film was obtained after drying at 60 °C for 6 h. Obviously, the film thickness can be easily tuned through controlling the repeat times.

For the conversion of BiOI to BiVO4, the procedure is based on the previous literature [10,35]. Briefly, The BiOI film (2 × 2 cm2) was completely covered by 40 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution containing 0.2 M vanadyl acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2) and heated in air at 450 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C/min. After that, the annealed film was soaking in 1 M NaOH to remove excess V2O5 present in BiVO4. The yellow BiVO4 film was finally obtained after several rinses with deionized water and drying under N2 flow.

The BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were synthesized by a facile solution impregnation route. The above BiVO4 films were immersed into to precursor solution with different concentration from 0.03 M to 0.35 M at room temperature for 12 h, then the BiVO4 photoanodes were washed by distilled water for several times and dried at 70 °C for 2 h. Finally the BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were fabricated.

Co-Pi was deposited on BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI via electrodeposition method in a three-electrode electrochemical workstation [36,39]. Briefly, the electrolyte is 0.5 mM Co(NO3)2 dissolved in 0.1 M KH2PO4 buffer solution at pH 7. The prepared BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were taken as the working electrode, Pt as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode (saturated), respectively. The electrodeposition was conducted at a bias of 1.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 10 min.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of these photoanodes were collected to examine the crystal structure with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) operated at 45 KV and 200 mA. The field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, SU8200, Japan, 3 KV) was developed to investigate the morphology and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2800, 200 KV) was carried out to look into the internal microstructure of samples. UV–vis spectra were performed on UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, PerkinElmer) ranged from 300 nm to 800 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on Axis Ultra, Kratos Analytical Ltd. with a standard monochro-matic Al Kα source and the binding energy of all elements were corrected using C 1s photoelectron peak at 284.8 eV. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra were obtained on Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi.

The photoelectrochemical measurement of all prepared photoanodes were conducted in a typical three-electrode configuration with Ag/AgCl (saturated) as reference and Pt wire as counter electrode by using an electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument, CHI 760E). The electrolyte is 0.5 M KH2PO4 without and with 1 M Na2SO3 aqueous solution. The light source through the samples (front-side illumination) was applied by a 500 W xenon lamp (Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd., CEL-S500) and the power density of the incident light was calibrated to 100 mW cm−2 under simulated AM 1.5 G filter and the illuminated area was controlled to be 0.25 cm−2 by a quartz window. The J–V curves were monitored by sweeping the potential to the positive direction range from -0.6 to 0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl with a scan rate of 10 mV s-1. The incident photo-to-current efficiency (IPCE) was measured at 1.23 VRHE by using the same three-electrode cell as mentioned above. The light source at a certain wavelength with a 20 nm bandpass was obtained by using a motorized monochromator (CEL-IS151) with a light source provided by a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300) and the power density was measured by a power meter (CEL-NP2000−2). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were obtained at frequency range from 10 Hz to 100 KHz and 1.23 VRHE under illumination of AM 1.5 G simulated solar light (100 mW cm−2). Mott-Schottky (M–S) plots were measured in 0.5 M KH2PO4 with 1 M Na2SO3 at a frequency of 1 KHz under dark condition. The electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of the photoanode was evaluated by measuring the cyclic voltammograms at different scan rate of 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mV s-1, respectively.

All potentials of photoelectrodes vs. Ag/AgCl are converted to potential vs. RHE using the Nernst equation:

C

is the electric double layer capacitance (μF cm−2), dφ/dt is the scan rate (mV s-1).

The synthesis of BiVO4 photoanode is based on the conversion of BiOI, the specific procedure is depicted in scheme S1. Firstly, the near transparent thin BiOI seed layer was formed on the FTO substrate by the spin coating technique through the series change of the chemical composition as shown in reaction 10 and 11 (Schemes S1c and S1d). Next, to obtain a dense and uniform BiOI film, precursor solution with high concentration was spin coated onto the BiOI seed layer and subsequently transformed to BiOI by water vapor treatment, as shown in Scheme S1e and S1f. The typical SEM image and XRD pattern of BiOI film is demonstrated in Figure S1. Clearly, it is composed of uniform nanoflakes and all the diffraction peaks can be well indexed to BiOI tetragonal structure (JCPDS 10−0445) and no other impurity peaks can be observed (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the precursor with “V” source was added in BiOI film, and the BiVO4 photoanode was then prepared after the calcination at high temperature. The BiVO4-BiOI was acquired by further keeping BiVO4 sample in BiOI precursor solution for a certain period of time and drying under low temperature. The quantity of BiOI can be modulated by controlling the impregnation time and the solution concentration.
(10)
B i
(
N
O
3
)
3
+ H O C

I

→ B i

2
O H

The typical morphology and film thickness of as-prepared BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Fig. 1(a) and (c)), which shows the homogenous feature of both films with many pores exist due to the template effect of BiOI nanoflakes. Further, from the enlarged insert image, one can see that the BiVO4 film consists of nano-worm like particles with the diameter around 150−200 nm. After the addition of the BiOI, a layer of thin nanoflakes can be observed, which is tightly adhere to the surface of the BiVO4 nanoparticles. From the cross section images shown in Fig. 1(b) and d, one can confirm that the BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI samples possess the similar film thickness of 400 nm, demonstrating the solution impregnation with pH 0.5 shows neglected influence on the morphology of BiVO4 film. The crystallographic structure of the BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI samples were investigated by XRD as shown in Fig. 1(e). The XRD patterns of the two films are both consistent well with the BiVO4 monoclinic structure (JCPDS 14−0688), and no other impurity peak can be detected except the diffraction peaks of FTO substrates. Moreover, the typical diffraction peaks of BiOI were not detected, which could be due to the relatively low amount. To evaluate the influence of BiOI on the adsorption of BiVO4, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI films are detected and displayed in Fig. 1(f). Compared to the BiVO4 sample, the absorbance of BiVO4-BiOI shows a slight increase in the region of 400−450 nm. However, the two samples exhibit the same absorption edge at around 480 nm, corresponding the bandgap of about 2.5 eV. This result is well consistent with the data reported in the previous literatures [10]. To investigate the crystalline of BiVO4 and BiOI, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images for BiVO4-BiOI sample were collected. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the BiVO4 nanoparticles are wrapped by BiOI nanosheets and the distribution of Bi (Fig. 2(e)), V (Fig. 2(f)) and I (Fig. 2(g)) elements provide more intuitive evidence, which is consistent well with the SEM images for BiVO4-BiOI (Fig. 1(c)). Fig. 2(b) and (c) are the HRTEM images of the red and yellow framed areas in Fig. 2(a). The crystal lattice with interplanar spacing of 0.47 nm and 0.26 nm could be assigned to (110) plane of BiVO4 (JCPDS 14−0688) and (111) plane of BiOI (JCPDS 10−0445), respectively, further confirming the formation of the heterosturcture.

To clarify the surface composition and chemical state of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. For comparison, pure BiOI nanosheets on FTO fabricated by the same preparation steps were also analyzed. The binding energies of all chemical elements were referenced for sample charging by using C 1s peak. Two typical spin-orbit splitting peaks displayed in Fig. 3 (a) can be assigned respectively to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+ in BiVO4 and BiOI. The typical peaks of V 2p can be fitted into two peaks located at 516.9 eV and 516.5 eV, which is consistent with the reported values of V5+ (ca.516.9 to 517.7 eV) and V4+ (ca.516 to 516.6 eV) [25,30,31]. The content of V4+ in BiVO4-BiOI is 42.1 % which is distinctly higher than that of 29.2 % in BiVO4 sample (Table S1).When further comparing the peak intensities of Bi3+ and V5+ in BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI, the signal of Bi3+ shows neglected difference, but the peak intensity of V5+ in BiVO4-BiOI sample is much weaker than pure BiVO4 sample, which could be due to modification layer of BiOI on the top of BiVO4 nanoparticles, reducing the intensity of the V5+. The typical doublet peaks shown in Fig. 3(c) is I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2in BiVO4-BiOI and BiOI samples. The broad peak located at 630.4 eV in pure BiVO4 could be resulted from the residual iodine during the conversion of BiOI to BiVO4. The chemical states of surface oxygen is depicted in Fig. 3(d), and the O 1s spectrum of three samples can be fitted into two peaks, corresponding to the lattice oxygen (OL) and oxygen vacancy (Ov) for BiVO4 [32,33], BiVO4-BiOI and BiOI. The specific relative contents of lattice oxygen and oxygen vacancy in three samples are summarized in Table S1. Distinctively, the content of oxygen vacancy is markedly increased in BiVO4-BiOI comparing with the pure BiVO4 and BiOI, directly hinting that oxygen vacancies in bulk of BiVO4 greatly increases upon the heterostructuring with BiOI. Comparing the redox characteristics of I− and V5+ (VO2 +/VO2+, EѲ = 0.991 V vs. NHE; I2/I−, EѲ = 0.54 V vs. NHE) and combining the XPS results of V and O, one can conclude that the increased oxygen vacancies in BiVO4 could be caused by the reduction of V5+ to V4+ by I−.

To evaluate the carrier transport properties of BiVO4-BiOI, the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) characterizations were employed to clarify the band structures of the BiVO4 and BiOI. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the corresponding absorption spectra present the absorption edges are approximate 490 nm and 570 nm for BiVO4 and BiOI, respectively. The optical band gap of the semiconductor can be estimated by the following equation [34]:
(12)
α h v =
A ( h v -
E g
)
n 2

Here,
α indicates the optical absorption coefficient, h v represents the photo energy,
E g
means the band gap and A is a proportionality constant. The n value is 1 for direct transition of BiVO4 and 4 for indirect transition of BiOI [28]. According to the Tauc plots depicted in Fig. 4(b), the Eg was calculated to be 2.53 eV and 1.90 eV for the BiVO4 and BiOI respectively, which is consistent well with previous reports [10,26]. The valence band energy (Ev ) can be calculated by subtracting the width of the UPS spectra (Fig. 4(c)) from the excitation energy of 21.21 eV and then converted to electrochemical energy potential in volts according to the reference standard of 0 V versus RHE. The Ev of BiVO4 and BiOI was 2.59 eV and 1.78 eV respectively, and the conduction band energy Ec can be acquired from Ev –Eg [37]. The corresponding band positions of Ec and Ev for BiVO4 and BiOI were depicted in Fig. 4(d), and the redox potential of water and oxidation potentials of sulfite were also marked for comparison.

To investigate the effect of BiOI nanosheets on photoelectrochemical performance of BiVO4 photoanode, the photocurrent-potential (J–V) curves (Fig. 5 (a)) of both BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were measured using a three-electrode cell configuration in 0.5 M KH2PO4 with and without the addition of 1 M Na2SO3.To optimize the quantity of BiOI, different amounts of BiOI on BiVO4 photoanodes were fabricated by controlling the concentration of the solution for impregnation. As shown in Fig. S2, compared to pristine BiVO4 photoanodes, the sulfite oxidation photocurrent of these BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes increased by 7.2 %, 7.9 %, 14.6 % and 23.4 % at 1.0 VRHE respectively and showed a positive correlation with solution concentration. When the concentration of precursor solution is 0.35 M, BiVO4-BiOI photoanode exhibits the best performance. Further increasing the concentration of the solution will cause the formation of inhomogeneous film. In addition, to evaluate the impact of immersing environment on the BiVO4 film, the experiment data of BiVO4 photoanode in the pure solvent solution (the mixed solution of ethylene glycol and acetic acid with the volume ration 2:1) and pure acetic acid solution are shown in Figs. S2e and S5, respectively. Both results showed that the pure solvent and acetic acid immersions exhibit neglected influence on the PEC performance of BiVO4 photoanode. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the sulfite oxidation photocurrent of the BiVO4 photoanode is around 3.03 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE under AM 1.5 G 100 mW cm−2 illumination, which is comparable to the results of previous reports [8,10,12]. After the modification of BiOI nanosheets, the sulfite oxidation photocurrent of BiVO4-BiOI can reach up to 3.88 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE. To further determine the contribution of BiOI nanosheets to the photocurrent, the incident photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was performed as a function of incident light wavelength at 1.23 VRHE. As displayed in Fig. 5(c), the IPCE of the BiVO4-BiOI is obviously higher than that of the pure BiVO4 in the wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 500 nm, manifesting the improvement of effective utilization of captured photons and the inhibited carriers’ recombination in the bulk or on the surface.

To get deeper insight of the carrier transport nature of these two photoanodes, the η bulk and η suface of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes were calculated. According to the absorption results shown in Fig. 1(f), the J abs was calculated to be 6.19 mA cm−2 and 6.20 mA cm−2 for BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI, respectively. The η bulk are plotted in Fig. 5(e) and the plot of η bulk for BiVO4-BiOI shows a rapid increase and much higher value in the potential ranging from 0.3 VRHE to 1.3 VRHE. The η bulk of BiVO4-BiOI photoanode can achieve 65.9 % at 1.23 VRHE, which increases around 27 % comparing to the 51.9 % of bare BiVO4 photoanode. Moreover, the water oxidation photocurrents for the two photoanodes were also measured and the η surface was calculated as shown in Fig. S3. The η surface also increased from 10.8 % for BiVO4 to 13.4 % for BiVO4-BiOI at 1.23 VRHE, respectively. The relatively low surface separation efficiency could be assigned to the poor catalytic nature of the BiVO4 and BiOI for water oxidation. This improvement of η bulk and η surface demonstrates the oxygen vacancies in BiVO4 and the built-in electric field existing in the BiVO4-BiOI heterojunction could simultaneously promote the bulk and surface carrier dynamics of BiVO4.

To figure out the carrier dynamics of the photoanodes of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and the Mott-Schotty (M–S) measurement were implemented. The EIS results of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI were analyzed by using equivalent circuit model as shown in Fig. 5(b). Rs represents series consistence which originates from the interface between the FTO substrate and bulk BiVO4 or BiVO4-BiOI; Rct indicates charge transfer resistance across the electrode-electrolyte interface [1,38,39]. The calculated Rs and Rct values are shown in Table S2. Clearly, the Rs values of BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes present very small difference, while the Rct value of BiVO4-BiOI (1419 Ω) is much smaller than that of BiVO4 (1751 Ω), and the same change trend of R ct value is obtained when measured in electrolyte with Na2SO3 as shown in Fig. S6 and Table S3, hinting the BiOI modification can significantly improve the charge transfer properties and enhance the PEC performance. Moreover, as exhibited in Fig. 5(d), the M–S plots of the BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes both perform positive slope, revealing that the coupling of BiVO4 and BiOI could not change the n-type semiconductor nature characteristic of BiVO4. The carrier densities gained from the BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI plots are 4.57 × 1020 cm−3 and 6.22 × 1020 cm−3, respectively. Evidently, the modification of BiOI can promote the donor density due to the efficient separation of photo-generated carriers. What’s more, the intercept values of the M–S plots to the x-axis can be used to evaluate the flatband position (E fb) of photoanode, the photocurrent onset potential for sulfite oxidation with fast oxidation kinetics is theoretically very close to the E fb. Compared to the pristine BiVO4, the E fb of the BiVO4-BiOI exhibits a negative shift of 12 mV, which consists well with the J–V curves shown in Fig. 5(a).

The stability of photoanode is another important factor for its practical application. As shown in Fig. 5(f), 5h-continuous sulfite oxidation measurement for BiVO4 and BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes at 0.8 VRHE in fresh 0.5 M KH2PO4 with 1 M Na2SO3 for 5 runs under AM 1.5 G irradiation was carried out. Notably, in the one-hour uninterrupted test, the photocurrent value of the two samples decreased by approximately 5%, but the initial value was restored after replacing the fresh electrolyte. This could be due to the reduced sulfite concentration near the photoanode. After 5-h-continuous sulfite oxidation measurement, the photocurrent densities of BiVO4-BiOI and BiVO4 retain 95 % and 86 % of the initial value respectively, indicating the decoration of BiOI is favorable for the long-term stability of BiVO4 photoanode.

To evaluate the PEC performance of a semiconductor photoanode, the superior electrical conductivity property, appropriate band gap and higher carrier separation and utilization efficiency for photocatalytic reaction would be expected. According to the aforementioned characterization and analysis, the decoration of BiOI nanosheets on BiVO4 would dramatically enhance the water splitting performance of the photoanode, which can be ascribed to the following two aspects. Firstly, plenty of oxygen vacancies were introduced into the BiVO4 with increasing the majority carrier density and improve the bulk charge separation efficiency due to the reduction of iodide ion. Additionally, the oxygen vacancies can afford unsaturated active sites for chemical reaction [40], and the measured electrochemical active surface area shown in Fig. S4 can provide an evidence for this view. Clearly, the slope (see Fig. S4) of BiVO4-BiOI film (478 μF cm−2) is distinctly higher than that of BiVO4 film (378 μF cm−2), which suggests the BiVO4-BiOI film can provide more active sites during the electrochemical process [5,39]. Secondly, owing to the proper band matching of the BiVO4 and BiOI, the BiVO4-BiOI heterojunction was formed and the built-in electric field could drive the separation of the photo excited electrons and holes. The holes diffuse to the BiOI to participate in oxidation of water and the electrons collected in BiVO4 then conducted to the counter electrode Pt through an external circuit to engage the water reduction reaction and the production of hydrogen. Overall, the introduced plenty of oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 through the reduction of V5+ to V4+ by I- and the built-in electric field in BiVO4-BiOI could synergistically improve the carrier dynamics of BiVO4, thereby having great potential to improve the PEC performance.

In summary, we present a facile solution impregnation method at room temperature to in-situ synthesize ultrathin BiOI nanosheets while introduce abundant oxygen vacancies into BiVO4. The photocurrent density of BiVO4-BiOI photoanode (3.88 mA cm−2) shows a significant improvement than that of pure BiVO4 photoanode (3.03 mA cm−2) at 1.23 VRHE. The bulk separation efficiency for BiVO4-BiOI can achieve 65.9 % at 1.23 VRHE, which increases around 27 % comparing to the 51.9 % of bare BiVO4. Further investigations reveal that the enhanced PEC performance can be summarized as two aspects. First, abundant oxygen vacancies were introduced into BiVO4 through the reduction of V5+ to V4+ by I-. Second, the built-in electric field in BiVO4-BiOI heterojunction can effectively promotes the separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers. The introduced oxygen vacancies in the bulk of BiVO4 and the built-in electric field in BiVO4-BiOI could synergistically improve the bulk and surface carrier dynamics of BiVO4.We believe that our facile and green strategy of solution impregnation paves an alternative way to the development of highly efficient BiVO4-BiOI photoanodes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. 3102019JC005, 3102019ghxm004, GK201702007 and G2017KY0002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51872179, 51911530212, 51872240, 51672225, and 11811530635), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2017JM5028 and 2020JM-273), the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing (NPU), China (Grant No. 2019-QZ-03) and the 1000 Youth Talent Program of China.
